# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Uplifting movie where female character with mental health struggles gets their act together

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Hi all,

Looking for some movie as in the title.

I would love to see a movie with perhaps bit quirky female lead who goes on quest of self improvement as that is what I am trying to do myself :) 

Edit: I have seen Girl Interrupted (1999) few years ago and loved it! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wild (2014) 
  - Was going to post this. Reese Witherspoon looking to clean up her druggie and sex addict life .
2. Not a movie so apologies, but the series Somebody Somewhere! 
3. Kiki's Delivery Service 
4. Frances ha 
5. Colossal
6. Silver Linings Playbook, Welcome to Me
7. I feel like I recommend this movie in this sub once a week but seriously: Brittany Runs a Marathon.
8. 28 days
9. The Outrun is about alcoholism but fits this model and is excellent 

## 2. Any movies with the same feel as Sister Act?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I just watched it and its sequel for the first time, and fell in love. They REALLY don’t make them like they used to. 

Doesn’t have to focus on religion, I’m just trying to find something with the same soul as sister act.

Sister Act is fast paced, entertaining, exciting, and uplifting. Whoopi KILLS it.

I want something that will make me laugh, but still bring a tear to my eye. I love movies where an outsider comes in and brings joy. The film is such a heartwarming portrayal of friendship. I loved the scene where the nuns come together to rescue Whoopi’s character

Sorry if this thread is chaotic and unfocused. I can’t quite come up with the appropriate words to say how I felt about the movie…I just know I loved it, and I’m not even religious. 

Are there any movies similar to Sister Act? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Full Monty
2. School of Rock
  - Came here to recommend this too! Perfect same vibe
3. Amelie: a lonely 20-something decides to make people's lives better ... secretly.

Hairspray: a fat girl joins a popular tv show and ends up integrating 60s Baltimore.

Harold and Maude: a lonely 18 y/o boy meets an old woman who shakes up his life.

The Intern: a retired CEO returns to work as an e-commerce intern and mentors everyone.

Dead Poets Society: a new English teacher teaches prep school boys about life.

First Wives Club: not an outsider-coming-in story, but similar vibes.

9 to 5: ditto.
4. Try another great Whoopi film, Jumping Jack Flash. Not quite the same feel, but one of her funniest performances.
  - “Mick, Mick, Mick! Speak ENGLISH!” 

A favorite
5. School of Rock maybe.
6. Little Miss Sunshine 
7. Interesting side fact- Whoopi’s role was meant for Bette Midler, but she turned it down bc she thought her fans didn’t want to see her play a nun
8. To Wong Foo

## 3. Animated films like The Last Unicorn?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m looking for some ethereal and fantasy animated films like The Last Unicorn. I love animated films that feel like it’s in a fairy world with unicorns, swans, faeries, knights and princesses. I’m sure there are some hidden gems out there.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Spirited away is a fantasy lil different to the last unicorn also I would try wizards is a good film
  - I love studio ghibli! 
2. Little Nemos Adventures in Slumberland

Secret of Nimh
3. Princes and Princesses

Son of the White Mare 

Irish Folklore Trilogy (Secret of Kells, Song of the Sea, Wolfwalkers)

Sirocco and the Kingdom of Winds
4. The Black Cauldron

Wizards (kind of)

The Flight of Dragons 
5. The Hobbit (1977) has the same animators as the last unicorn

It's not animated but Legend has the same whimsical retro vibe and has unicorns and fairies 

The Dark Crystal, also not animated, but all the characters are Jim Henson puppets, very whimsical and retro high fantasy vibes 
6. Not animated, but have you seen Legend? Otherwise someone else recommended Cartoon Saloons works, Secret of Kells, Song of the Sea, Wolfwalker, and The Breadwinner are great! The same folks who did the last unicorn also did The Hobbit and Return of the King cartoons (amongst many others). 
7. Flight of Dragons (1982)
8. Fire and Ice
9. Ferngully: The Last Rainforest was a favourite of mine in the 1990s. Has fairies.

## 4. Classic horror films

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I just saw a series of great classic thriller films and I need more recommendations. Knife in the Water, Cul-de-Sac, Possession, Repulsion, Rosemary's Baby, The Tenant, Persona, Hour of the Wolf. The films can be from any time frame but newer films just can't compete with the classics. I love the vibe of the old films and if anyone has suggestions, you're most welcome to recommend. Thanks a lot.

Edit: just want to add that it doesn't necessarily have to be a horror or outright creature feature. Most of the films I mentioned have underlying discomfort or thrill but they are all expertly done by master filmmakers. I'm specifically looking for classy films from the 60's, 70's, 80's that have the vibe of Polanski and Bergman. My favourite is Cul de sac and Knife in the water and neither is horror.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Suspiria (1977) 
2. Don’t Look Now (1973)
3. The Changeling (1980)

The Fly (both 1958 and 1986!)
4. The Exorcist
5. Night of the Hunter

Spider Baby
6. Scanners and Videodrome. Neither are traditional horrors but you can’t go wrong with Cronenberg body-horror. And I’d absolutely say they’re both good watches now - not “good for the time” or anything. 
7. The Invisible Man (1933)

Great script, production design and ensemble cast, but Claude Rains’ performance is a masterpiece.
8. Carnival of Souls
9. Invasion of the Body Snatchers (both 1956 and 1978 versions).
10. The People Under The Stairs (1991)

## 5. On the edge thrillers

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Hey guys, most of the movies and TV shows I’ve watched were from Spain, like The Body, The Innocent (series), Secret in Their Eyes, Invisible Guest, etc. 

Unfortunately, and fortunately, I’ve seen the most mainstream, famous movies in that genre. Is there anything you can still recommend that brings so much thrill and makes you not blink? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Duel
2. Marshland

Sleep Tight

Kidnapped

The Guilty
3. Calibre (2018)
4. Dead Calm

Miracle Mile

The Wages of Fear / Sorcerer

Only the Animals

The Last Seduction

5. The films you named are all class, some of the best films made. Especially Secret in their eyes which I think is a modern day classic. 
6. Locke - it’s pretty thrilling for a film about a guy just driving and talking on the phone
7. Sexy Beast
8. Eden Lake (2008)   - this one fucked me up, I spent all night thinking about it lmao 

Perfect Blue

The Game

What Lies Beneath

The Invasion (2007)

A Perfect Getaway

Prisoners

Contagion 

Blink Twice

Vantage point

Hotel Mumbai

No Escape

The Pelican Brief

The Wave (2015)
9. Cant say anything about 'not blink' part but watched 'Apex'. Kinda liked it. Didn't get bored or anything.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Willem Dafoe in Boondock Saints is absolutely hilarious and fun to watch

**Score:** 3535
**Body:** The Boondock Saints is a cult classic I’ve rewatched many times.

But man… Willem Dafoe  just has so much fun with the role, you can’t help but grin! His moments where he gets unhinged lives rent free in my head.

An amazing actor all around and his one liners always make me lol.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "For a few seconds, this place was Armageddon. AND THERE WAS A FIRE FIGHT!"


Thanks for reminding me btw, i was thinking of rewatching it lately 😀
  - “What if it was one guy with six guns??”

😂😂😂

You’re very welcome!! 💯💯
    - Better than being serial crushed by some huge friggin guy. 
2. ![gif](giphy|GdrxhQGjYARPi)
3. I just wanted to cuddle.
  - “Well… Freud was right.”
4. *Well, we'll have to check with your mom. But it's okay with me if your friends sleep over*

Every single line in that movie is quotable, and it never gets old. The acting from everyone was phenomenal, especially DaFoe. 
5. "Now that Duffy has relinquished his King Bonehead crown, I see we have an heir to the throne!"

https://preview.redd.it/oo02px1qdkxg1.jpeg?width=1079&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=361e9ec0501dca6153721222a288d65f3472c664
  - “The word you’re looking for was symbolism!! What is the ssssssyymbolism there…”
6. Hes what made this movie so entertaining 

## 2. I’m rewatching The Avengers movies and it would have a far better story ending if Captain America had been the one to have died, sacrificing himself for the greater good. He was a soldier and a man out of his time.

**Score:** 588
**Body:** He was also a more moralistic and selfless hero than Tony Stark. He was worthy of Mjölnir and one of the closest Avengers to being innocent (like Vision and Peter Parker). Thus, his death would have felt like a much deeper tragedy than Tony’s. The “on your left” rally scene was the perfect lead up for this. 

I love Iron Man and the Avengers movies but, I don’t see why they didn’t sacrifice the Cap if they were just going to pass on the mantle to someone else and have RDJ return anyway.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tony's sacrifice shows growth. Steve would probably have sacrificed himself in his first showing. 
  - Steve was willing to die during *training* by diving on that grenade (had it been live) 
    - Steve was willing to die and Stark was living his best life at the beginning of their arcs


Now Steve has to live his best life and Stark is willing to die
  - Agreed. Steve’s character arc (change) was becoming selfish for once, leaving the shield and Captain America behind, and living a life.  Tony was the opposite. He went from selfish to making the sacrifice. 
    - Honestly, such good writing to give a character arc that gives the "perfect boyscout"  a traditionally negative trait. Twisting it into something earned rather than be in his nature was nice to see
      - That was the thing about it too, he wasn’t just fuck y’all, I’m out, you guys deal with it. He earnt his rest and what he wanted
  - Steve literally sacrifices himself in his first movie. Its just that he's a super soldier and survived it.
2. He sacrificed himself too. 

For that ass. 
3. Iron Man dying completes one of the best character arcs, and Steve got to go back and live his life.  That seems like a better ending to me.  You know... a happy one?
4. That’s the whole point. Cap is the obvious choice; he always was. So therefore making Tony the one to sacrifice himself, a character, who, in the first film was living for himself and self-centered, grows  and culminates his character arc in the infinity saga by sacrificing himself at the end. I think it was the right choice and candidly, I don’t believe the producers thought they were going to bring RDJ back. That was really the product of a floundering couple phases that did not live up to the infinity Saga phase.

## 3. One of the most underrated Actors: James McAvoy

**Score:** 568
**Body:** When talking about actors who are underrated, overlooked, or simply not talked about enough, my mind immediately goes to James McAvoy.

I recently rewatched Split, and it reminded me once again how insanely talented this man is and how strangely underappreciated he still feels in Hollywood. After reading a similar post about Edward Norton and how he's also one of Hollywood's most underappreciated figures, I felt like adding one of my favorite actors to the discussion. 

Of course this is primarily my personal and subjective opinion. I’m sure some of you may disagree or have arguments against and for my point, so you're welcome to join the discussion in the comments. Go ahead and explain why I might be wrong, or who you think is also underappreciated—or even overrated.

So with that mentioned, fair warning: 

\*\*You are about to witness an unreal case of glazing.\*\*

The history of characters this man has embodied, and the range he has shown throughout his career, should not be overlooked or brushed aside. He has proven again and again that he is not just a “good actor,” but an actor with real versatility, dedication, and the ability to completely disappear into a role. And one of the most impressive things about his career is that he has somehow managed to avoid being typecast.

A good example of typecasting would be Seann William Scott after playing Stifler in American Pie. He did such a good job — maybe even too good — that audiences and casting directors struggled to see him as anything else. He became strongly associated with that loud, unserious, comedic persona, and even when he wanted to move into more serious roles, it felt like the industry was not really willing to give him that chance.

James McAvoy could have easily fallen into a similar trap. Many people know him as the younger Charles Xavier from the X-Men films, others may associate him with Wesley Gibson from Wanted, Robbie Turner from Atonement, Mr. Tumnus from The Chronicles of Narnia, or Victor Frankenstein. But despite being part of several recognizable films and characters, he never got stuck in one specific lane.

Instead, he built an incredibly diverse filmography, moving convincingly between drama, comedy, action, fantasy, and horror — from Atonement and Wanted to Split and Speak No Evil. And that is where his talent becomes especially obvious.

Horror movies often benefit from casting lesser-known or rising actors, partly because of budget, but also because the audience does not bring too much “actor baggage” into the film. You usually do not want viewers to look at the main character or mysterious antagonist and immediately think of another famous role. Horror works best when the film feels like its own isolated world, and the character on screen feels like a real person inside that world, not just a famous actor in a new costume.

With James McAvoy, that somehow is not a problem. Even though he has played very recognizable characters, he has enough range and intensity to make each performance feel completely separate. When you watch him in Split or Speak No Evil, you are not thinking about X-Men, Atonement, Wanted, or Narnia. You are locked into that specific character and that specific world. That, to me, is proof of how strong and versatile he is as an actor.

Some actors are extremely successful while still operating within a very familiar lane. Jason Statham, Dwayne Johnson, and Vin Diesel are obvious examples — and that is not meant as an insult, because they clearly know what their audience wants, and they are very good at what they do. But their characters often feel like variations of the same screen persona.

That is why their scenes can sometimes blur together. You could cut together a bunch of random moments from several of The Rock’s recent movies, and it would be comedically hard to tell which scene comes from which film, because the performance, the energy, the personality, the body language, and even the type of character are often so similar. There is a reason why there are so many memes about actors who basically “play themselves” in every movie, with names like Statham, Diesel, and The Rock usually being among the first examples people mention.

McAvoy is the complete opposite of that. He never feels like he is just bringing “James McAvoy” into a new setting. He disappears into roles that feel distinct, specific, and fully realized, to the point where the actor’s own persona almost vanishes behind the character.

That is why I genuinely regard him as one of the strongest actors working today. It is honestly baffling to me that he has not received more major award recognition, especially from the Oscars. He has had BAFTA and Golden Globe recognition, sure, but for the level of range, commitment, and transformation he has shown over the years, it still feels like Hollywood has not fully given him the credit he deserves.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would say he's rated well.
  - Especially on Reddit, where most people have a huge admiration for superhero movies. Sometimes I’m honestly pretty confused, I mean, I have a hard time understanding Redditors when they take apart Avatar or The Lord of the Rings, yet at the same time call Deadpool & Wolverine or Dark Phoenix flawless masterpieces. People on that platform are hard to follow.
2. Last king of Scotland?
3. It saddens me that nobody on Reddit seems to understand what the word underrated means. 
  - Underrated comment
4. I've been a fan since I saw him in the Children of Dune mini-series.
5. He was great on Filth.
6. Atonement. That’s all I have to say.
7. I first saw him in back-to-back awards-contending movies in 2006-07, *Last King of Scotland* and *Atonement*. In both cases, he was very good in a big role, but co-stars got more of the attention. 

I thought he'd be a regular fixture in historicals and classy period pieces for many falls/winters to come. But his career went in more of a popular direction with movies like *Wanted*, the X-Men films, *It: Chapter Two*, and the two Shyamalan collaborations (the first of which was one of MNS's better films). 

McAvoy is compelling and charismatic and can do a lot of different things.
8. Thanks chat gpt.

## 4. Thoughts, opinions. I’m about to rewatch this.

**Score:** 424

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|gQIs18OINSq9a)
  - https://preview.redd.it/nh42a81i0hxg1.jpeg?width=1179&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=f6954ddfbe0a416dc77047efd77e0ba9c42d3350

There Will be Kindness
2. The fact this and No Country came out in the same year, insane. Two American masterpieces. 
  - And that they both were filmed outside Marfa TX. In fact, I read the big rig fire caused a disturbance for NCFOM filming due to the smoke 
3. Masterpiece. 
4. The music makes it so intense with incredible acting 
5. Elite, 10/10 unironically without caveats. You’ll watch 1000 movies trying to recapture the feelings this film evokes. Watch Gangs of New York the next week to fill the void

## 5. Your opinions about "Ronin" (1998)?

**Score:** 292
**Body:** I watched it for the first time a couple years ago. I just rewatched it again last night and love it. The story is very intense and has so many twists and turns and very interesting about one mystery employer hiring 5 stranger guys from different countries to do one specific job in Paris. The cast is excellent especially Robert De Niro and Jean Reno both had a great chemistry together. I think the highlight of the movie is the car chase through Paris which is so exciting and well made without any shaky cam or quick cut. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I absolutely love this movie.  The concept of a bunch of ex spies trying to find something to do post Cold War plus incredible chase scenes, great acting…incredible movie.
2. One of the greatest “men with particular set of skills” movies with one of the greatest car chase scenes for sure
  - And Natascha McElhone… 
3. yeah, probably #1 car chase for me. The briefcase must be one of the best mcguffins in cinema. Also liked the ronin legend scene
4. *What's the color of the boathouse at Hereford?*
  - How the fuck should I know
  - *There is no boathouse. Why, you're not from North Kilt Town at all!*
5. No Ronin no Bourne 
  - I'm kind of pissed I haven't made that connection with how long I've been a fan
6. It's just a Fucking Great Movie. Endlessly rewatchable. Anyone who comments in this thread has probably seen it at least 10 times. And is probably a dude. And understands that that there's something incommunicable but special about Ronin.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I just watched Matewan (1987) by John Sayles.

**Score:** 70
**Body:** It has truly been a while since I watched a film that was able to get to me as much as *Matewan* (1987). It is John Sayles' most well known work, about a union organiser who is sent to a small mining town in West Virginia in 1920, and has to help its workers against an exploitive company and the private detectives hired to break them, based on the real life Matewan massacre of 1920.  
Matewan is a very emotional film, not in the sense that it will leave you in tears, but in that it is very good at setting off strong emotions within the viewer. A major theme of the film is unfairness, Sayles makes the audience watch as the workers and townspeople of Matewan are taken advantage of, how they are mistreated and even wrongfully antagonised. It was maddening to watch.

In contrast, major reoccurring point in the film is the relations between the three different groups that make of the workers: the native townspeople, the Italian immigrants, and the recently arrived African-Americans. The film starts off with a lot of racial tensions between the three, the latter two start off being treated as unwelcome foreigners and scabs by the townspeople, as they were brought in by the mining company to work instead of the local union. As the film progresses the three all begin to connect, they all realise they are not so different, and they all have a common goal. This is primarily depicted through the relationship between Mrs. Elkins and Rosaria, as they start off distrustful of each other, not accepting the other's cooking, to at the end helping each other cook to feed their children. At the end, they were all people who just wanted to be allowed to live.

The detectives are some of the most vile antagonists I have seen in a film for a long time, their actors do a great job at making them so cruel and malign. What stuck out to me the most is how they were depicted with such open immorality, the detectives had no sense of honour or dignity for others, they mock faith, and use tactics of fear and force to keep the people repressed. The detectives represent the evil that keeps people down when they are too scared to stand up, they thrive on good people doing nothing, but become less powerful when good people like the sheriff, and the protagonist stand up to them. >!This all culminates in one of the most emotionally cathartic villain defeats I have seen in a long time.!<

Up until now in the post, I have mainly been discussing the story and writing of the film, one of the strongest aspects of Matewan is the cinematography. Sayles makes good use of lighting and consistent usage of visual darkness to give the various scenes feelings of warmth and others cold, or dangerous. Something that struck out to me was the usage of colours, and how in each scene they just go together in such a harmonic way.

But to go back to the point of emotion, I think one of the reasons this film is so competent at bringing out emotion, is that the mistreatment and hardship that the workers and townspeople endure in the film still exists in the modern world. The way their are repressed and treated by tools by companies who think they are unstoppable still persists, and as much as I would like to say that companies no longer hire private detectives, practically mercenaries, to break people who stand up, there are less fortunate parts of the world where that is still the case.

Overall, I am really impressed by how good and emotional of a film *Matewan* was, and I look forward to watching more films by John Sayles after this.  
What do you think of the film?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Matewan is a great and important film. The cinematography is by two-time Oscar winner, the late Haskell Wexler, ASC, for which he received a well-deserved Oscar nomination. It’s one of my favorites of his ouevre, and among his best work.

As an aside, while in production, Matewan itself began as a non-union production (all of Sayles’ efforts are independent features, and on the lower end of the budget scale). The crew actually went through their own union organizing effort, eventually getting under contract with NABET, not the dominant entertainment-worker union, IATSE. I’ve always wondered how that influenced the making of the film.
  - Just checked out the cinematographer, wow, he also worked on *In the Heat of Night*, *One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest*, and *Days of Heaven*, quite an impressive record, no wonder the cinematography was so good! It was definitely one of the main highlights of the film for me.

I was aware of Sayles being mainly am independent director, it was refreshing to watch an independent style film after the last few I have seen prior were more mainstream Hollywood films.
    - Wexler’s Oscars are for Who’s Afraid of Virginia Woolf? (1967, B&W), and Bound for Glory (1977, Color). Bound for Glory is the first use of Steadicam in a motion picture. He also shot American Graffiti with his own Eclair, though credited as “Visual Consultant” for union reasons. He deserved to share Nestor Almendros’ Oscar for Days of Heaven, but it was awarded solo to Almendros. He was a great filmmaker, humanitarian, and union man. He shot numerous documentaries and directed, too.
    - Check out the film Wexler directed himself, Medium Cool, a film set during the DNC riots of '68 (using actual footage Wexler shot on the day, with actors in the midst of the actual action)
      - *Medium Cool* was the film that first made me aware of the essential connection between cinematography and storytelling. Also the interplay between cinematography and editing.

I was in my late teens. Possibly those a-ha moments were in part because I had seen enough movies at that point that the light bulbs had started turning on, and I was always wondering "how does film actually work". But I've always felt that without seeing *Medium Cool* I wouldn't have put so much together. The look and mood of that movie had so much presence, and its interweaving of both fiction and documentary film showed me what they have in common, and how one can masquerade as the other.
2. Matewan was a major influence on me when I first saw it as a teenager on the big screen just a few years after its release. For those still discovering Sayles, I strongly recommend two other movies of his: Lone Star (1996) and Limbo (1999)—the latter also shot by Wexler.
3. I've had this on my watchlist since forever. My only exposure to Sayles is The Brother From Another Planet, but I've rewatched it so many times since then. Very underrated, and has similarities with your description. I guess Sayles specialty is human psychology and race & class relations in American society.

I don't know why his name is rarely mentioned, I'd put him among (or beyond) the likes of Linklater. I'd recommend to find any director's commentary for his films to rewatch too, I've found his explanation of his process to be very interesting. Definitely excited about exploring more of his work.
4. Oh man, Matewan is such an amazing film, definitely in my top 10 of all time. I would recommend checking out Lone Star (1996) next. I think those two are generally considered his best films. I just realized there’s a bunch of his films I haven yet seen; will have to get caught up. Tangential note: hadn’t made this connection before, but I’ve been watching the tv series The Wire for the first time, and much of what I like about The Wire is what I like about Sayles’ films: the ensemble cast of characters, intertwining stories, a focus on systemic injustice, political corruption, ordinary people caught up in the machinery of a broken system, and the compromises well-meaning people have to make to get some progress. Looks like Sayles has been focused on writing novels in recent years. I wonder if he’s done with directing?
  - 'City of Hope' by John Sayles probably had a huge influence on 'The Wire'. It's really good.
    - Oh cool, thanks! Haven’t seen that one yet. 

## 2. Inland Empire- "A Woman in Trouble"

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Throughout his career David Lynch has been fascinated with women in trouble and the "trouble" is always the evil and cruelty of men. Laura Palmer from Twin Peaks is the obvious example but also Dorothy from Blue Velvet, Lulu from Wild at Heart, Diane from Mulholland Drive and several other Twin Peaks characters depict women as victims but also with their own agency and strength at times. In Inland Empire, Lynch brings this theme to the forefront and examines it in fascinating ways.

In IE, Laura Dern plays three separate characters in my interpretation and it feels almost like a tribute to victimized women in all their different forms. The first character is Nikki Grace, a wealthy and successful Hollywood actress looking for a "comeback". She has a domineering and threatening husband and seems to be manipulated by the film industry, yes she earns the big role but there are secrets that are kept from her and the film production goes on even after it is clear there is something wrong with her mentally. The role is cursed by a supernatural being called the phantom who traps women in this tormenting story or a purgatorial hotel room. Unlike Diane from Mulholland Drive, Nikki has appears to have some success in the movies but is still in a position of weakness, desperate for a role and driven to insanity by the pressure. She eventually cheats on her husband, which she deserves blame for but she is also pressured by her costar, Devon(Justin Theroux) who has a reputation as a playboy.

When Nikki cheats on her husband and sees the writing Axxon-N on the wall outside the market, her personality splits, she loses the Nikki persona and fully transforms into the life of the character she is portraying; Susan Blue. Susan is a lower middle class housewife with a physically abusive husband and again is cheating with a character played by Theroux, Billy, who is now a rich man living in a mansion with his wife and child. During this segment of the film, Susan is accompanied and supported by a group of ghostly women (who may be victims of the same curse) who talk about their experiences with men, sexuality, and desperation to be accepted. Susan becomes pregnant with Billy's child and she is beaten by her husband and disposed of and and humiliated by Billy.

After being thrown away by both Billy and her husband, Dern goes though a major breakdown and again encounters the word Axxon-N, splitting into a third character (who I believe is never named). This character is broken down whore on the dirty streets of LA with a vast and bizarre history of traumatic experiences with men. The ghostly women return but reflect Dern's state as they are now streetwalkers who look beaten up and addicted to drugs. Dern's third character is eventually murdered by woman, but a woman who was hypnotized by the phantom (so even the killer is a victim of a man). She dies on the streets surrounded by homeless people, the only ones who show her any kind of compassion.

So through all these roles Nikki Grace is forced to live through various experiences of manipulation, violence, abuse and coercion exercised by men onto women in different socioeconomic and situational contexts. The last layer of this "character" is not even played by Dern, but is The Lost Girl a polish women from what seems to be the early 20th century is Poland. She seems to be married to the evil phantom, strays in her marriage and is cursed. She is either murdered or commits murder and is trapped in a interdimensional purgatory by the phantom. Just as Dern and the Lost Girl represent women in trouble, the phantom represents the patriarchal evil that control and mistreat women. At the end of the film, Dern learns all she needs about the history of the curse and how to breaks it and is able to exercise her strength and agency. She takes out the phantom and frees the Lost Girl, exerting control over the narrative and refusing to exist as the victim.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Axxon", spoken aloud, sounds a lot like "action", which a director calls to begin a scene.

The crying Polish girl in the film's framing devices is the "real" character in this movie. All of Dern's characters are roles that the Polish girl imagines herself in, within her fantasies of escape from her trouble. But the fantasies all go wrong too. There's no real escape in escapism.
2.  Inland Empire is Lynch's best film, imo. 

I remember watching it in the theater and just being stunned and oddly excited(?) by what I had just seen. Dern is a revelation throughout and Lynch had me like putty in his hands by the end; the dance number was pure catharsis rather than anything  mystifying. He was absolutely successful in transporting me to his wavelength.

## 3. People who love hearing directors commentary on physical media, what movies experience elevated them to next level.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Beware minor spoilers for few movies.Topic. I love when the physical discs include directors or any other additnal tracks featuring movie discussion. I recently was watching Marty Supreme Again and damn when u listen to the director's vision of why certain stuff was put in that particular way it blows my mind. For e.g Safdie has mentioned the reason for marty wearing glass and how exactly they made his eyes look small. I just love these details. Similarly The Korean oldboy directors commentary is just amazing. In the beginning itself when a shot of roads are shown he describes that it represents the linear nature of time. The whole Gangs of wasseypur Director commentary is so damn good it just elevates an already amazing movie to the next level. Many of Edward wright movies have very good directors track. One movie which has a very funny track is Armageddon by ben affleck . It just doesn't make any sense why none of the streaming services bother to have them , I mean it doesn't need to be a 5.1 Atmos track it can be the most basic audio compressed track.           

The commentary track available to download when the movie is in theatre for movies like knife out and   more recently project hail mary is such a welcome initiative .            

 Looking for more insight of people in this community whether they listen to them and if so what are there personal favorite. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You’ve alluded to it but it’s worth highlighting: Edgar Wright and Quentin Tarantino spend the entirety of Hot Fuzz’s commentary discussing obscure British cinema. Interesting if you’re into that sort of thing. 
2. The commentary for Talladega Nights of all movies is one of my favorites. Almost the entire cast is there, and they are basically doing an improv skit where they are all reuniting in the future after 25 years. Amongst the highlights: John C Reilly is now the leader of North Michigan, one of the territories that has been created during subsequent civil wars, and the director Adam McKay is acting as his son because Adam was killed in a very public shark attack. It was basically 2 hours of very funny people doing improv, pretty funny stuff.
3. I'm very curious about when people choose to listen to these. I am not sure I have ever listened to one, because for me movies usually exist in one of two states: either "I just watched this and do not want to spend another two hours immediately watching the same movie again with a different audio track, " and "I haven't seen this movie in a while and I want to watch the movie, not hear about the movie." 
  - I’ve yet to watch one either…but I always imagine I would do it a day or two later (when the movie is still fresh and after I’ve been analyzing it in my head), and when I haven’t seen it in a little while but I have some house chores to do and can’t give it my full attention (and I use it like a podcast).
  - Interesting, I have I think three categories.

* "I just watched this and that was enough."
* "I just watched this and want to hear a two hour discussion of it."
* "I just watched this and liked it so much I want to watch it again."

The second category is a shoe-in for an audio commentary rewatch.

The third category isn't very common. Sometimes it might lead to a second viewing with commentary, sometimes it might lead to a second viewing without commentary.

Two back-to-back viewings without commentary sometimes even lead to a third viewing with commentary. But watching a movie three times or more like this is pretty rare, I can only remember maybe a handful of those ever. Although it has been a long time since I got DVDs in the mail (last time I had regular access to commentaries), so hard to say for sure.
4. I remember I would put on the Species 2 commentary and let the German-accented director lull me to sleep with his random stories about being friends with Peter Boyle and really liking a Helena Bonham Carter movie. I guess it was early ASMR in a way.
5. The two (movies) that made me a fan of commentary tracks were Fight Club and Donnie Darko. It annoys the hell out of me that Fight Club doesn’t have the special features/extras with the digital purchase versions.
6. Streaming services don't have them because they want you to pay money and purchase the disc.

My favorite commentaries are not by directors. The Roger Ebert commentaries on Casablanca and Citizen Kane (and probably others) are incredibly informative. An unexpectedly good director commentary is on Speed. Jan de Bont is really engaging. 
7. I only listen to the commentary track if the movie is amazing and I don't mind rewatching it again within a few days and getting some new perspective or insight. The best ones are usually from the director and/or cast, and the worst ones IMO are usually from film historians who spend the whole movie talking about really dull background stuff. I forget the movie, but one track was this historian just listing every actor as they appeared on screen, listing their birthday and when they died, and then listing all their other film credits without any other deeper insight. 


The movie Narc from 2002 has an excellent commentary track with the film's director Joe Carnahan and the editor John Gilroy. Carnahan really gets into the depths of what he was wanting to achieve with the movie and is super funny and self depreciating too. If you like the movie, I highly recommend the commentary. 
8. I always wished that more movies would have more than just the one commentary track.

On some movies i would like a cast commentary track discussing their experiences, a director commentary on the themes, experiences, technical challenges, then more technical commentary from the production side. I feel that so many people work on the movie in a creative way and so much of the work they do just blends into the background when it comes to it being acknowledged.

## 4. A Man Escaped (1956 dir. Robert Bresson)

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I need some help with this film. I tend to get a little lost right after the introduction of Jost around 30-35 minutes from the end.The dialogues between him and Fontaine always strike me as a bit on the cryptic side.

For instance, why does Jost say, "It was for France. Why else would I sign up?" How does he think joining the German army will help France? (Also, he initially says he's in prison for stopping French people from getting on the Germans' trains, but later it seems like he's there for shooting a policeman.)

Later, Fontaine says, "You just had to come here," and Jost answers, "They're all gone." Who/what are all gone?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's been a while since I saw the film but with regards to signing up "for France", there were multiple collaborationist movements throughout Europe who supported the Nazis with the promise of some sort of national revival after the war (as well as shared fascist ideals, anti-Semitism and/or anti-communism).  



In France, the Vichy regime's motto was "Work, Family, Fatherland", so it would not be so strange to have a collaborationist join the Germans and claim it was "For France". Especially an impressionable young man like Jost.  


Within the film I think that statement is supposed to characterize him as slightly naive, not too smart. Someone who bought into the story that you could help your country by joining its invaders.

## 5. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (April 26, 2026)

**Score:** 4
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [**WEEK # 277:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

**REPENTANCE** (1987) is my 6th absurdist art film from the Republic of Georgia. It's a strange tale of a dictatorial small town mayor, sporting a tiny toothbrush mustache and always dressed in a Fascist black-shirt. At his funeral, he is mourned as a benevolent leader, but when his corpse starts being dug up repeatedly, the traumas of his reign of terror are being dug up as well. 

A clear rebuke of a Stalin-like leader, the film was banned in the Soviet Union, and had been recently re-assessed and re-issued. It has many surrealistic Buñuel-like scenes, and bizarre, dream-like logic overall, but it just didn't jell out completely for me. 

[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yYTAL58DAhc)

🍿 

One of my greatest joys watching an excessive number of movies is when, by utter coincidence, I discover a unique literary gem by a filmmaker I never ever heard of. Such is the surreal **THE MUSIC OF CHANCE** (1993) by new-to-me artist Philip Haas. It's an existential mystery-box about poker hustler James Spader and stoic fireman Mandy Patinkin who are betting it all on a game with a couple of eccentric millionaires. It's original and baffling and offers no simple explanations. Oh, and there's a large model town that the Joel Grey character is constructing upstairs, which, like the Hedge Maze in 'The Shining', is linked to the outcome of these two unlucky characters. Also, M. Emmet Walsh and Charles Durning play cryptic roles. 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. 
[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UF97Qun2NcE)

[The full movie is available for free on YouTube.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RYZT5ftJDmY) **Recommended!**

Completely different, but also adapted from a story by Paul Auster, **IT WAS MINE** (2015). A man is searching in many bookstores and libraries for a rare and remarkable book until, in a random coincidence, he runs into a woman who just finished reading it. It is a short Norwegian film, done in the [most original animated style.](https://vimeo.com/131189409) *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

**CRUEL TALE** (1930) is an obscure silent French horror film, directed by one Gaston Modot (Remembered for sucking on the toe of the statue in Buñuel’s 'L’Age d’Or'). It's set during the inquisition, where a tortured prisoner about to be burned at the stake is trying to escape from his dungeon. Nearly like Dreyer's 'The passion of Joan of Arc', [it's visually stunning:](https://www.cinematheque.fr/henri/film/50240-conte-cruel-gaston-modot-1930/) The play with lights and shadows, compositions, camera movements make this crisp, restored copy a special mood piece. 

🍿 

*“When this is all over, nobody will admit to ever having supported it.”*

After reading [the recent Atlantic essay](https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/2026/04/sorrow-and-pity-trump/686829/?gift=j9r7avb6p-KY8zdjhsiSZ6ZDW3lcisRHfj7Fe5hlowU) about **THE SORROW AND THE PITY**, I realized that I never actually saw Marcel Ophüls' seminal documentary (But I did see his 'Hôtel Terminus', also about the same painful French topic).

It examines the complex reasons how and why ordinary French citizens reacted to the Nazi regime during the 4 years of occupation. Some chose to join the resistance and suffer the consequences, but most accepted the situation as a fait accompli, and became complicit collaborators with the Germans. In 1969, at the time of release, showing this unvarnished point of view was highly controversial, because the country chose to remember its past as heroic. The 4+ hour long exposition shows that most people simply looked away and said nothing, even when terror and mass murders occurred next door. 

The lessons for today are spelled out in David A. Graham's article above.

It reminded me of two other long and fascinating documentaries, 'Occupied City' from 2023 about Amsterdam during WW2, and of course, Claude Lanzmann's 'Shoah'. 

🍿 

First watch: **MRS. MINIVER**, the highest grossing film of 1942, and winner of six academy awards. In spite of it being a Hollywood propaganda vehicle, it's actually a lovely inspirational story of how an "ordinary" (but very well-to-do) family manages to live during the first few years of Second World War in rural England. (The ordinary family, an architect, his housewife and their 3 children live in an enormous mansion...) It contains a very unexpected plot twist at the end, as well as a final nationalistic sermon from the pulpit about the fight for freedom and our 'way of life'. Also, one very surprising short scene where wife Greer Garson gets spanked by her husband, in jest - but for real.

Bonus: **FROM THE END OF THE EARTH** (1939) was part of MGM's "Romance of Celluloid" series, produced between 1937 and 1944. It's a self-promotional short about international shipping which quickly turns into a trailer for all the current 'Coming attractions'. Greer Garson has a cameo together with dozens of other stars of the day. 

🍿 

*"Tilda collects directors like Pokémon..."*

**THE MAN FROM LONDON** (2007), my 4th by Béla Tarr (AND his partner Ágnes Hranitzky). It's an artful and very slow Film Noir, Based on a novel by Georges Simenon. It tells of a railway switchman in a dark European port-town, who discovers a suitcase containing a large sum of money. The black and white cinematography and atmospheric locations in Corsica were gorgeous, but the fact that it was not played in Hungarian, but in an uncomfortable mixture of French and English was a distraction. Tilda Swinton played the unhappy, nagging wife. 4/10. 

🍿 

*"This turned out completely different from what we imagined."*

**THE GULLSPÅNG MIRACLE** (2023) is an intriguing Norwegian meta-documentary, with many twists and turns, which might have worked even better as a 'based on a true story drama'. 

Two old religious sisters from the northern part of Norway decide to buy an apartment 1,000 kilometers south in Sweden after an accident at an amusement park forces them to stay there for a long period of time. But when they enter the apartment, they find the exact painting hanging in the kitchen that one of the sisters had seen repeatedly in her dreams. They take it as a divine sign, and when they meet the seller to sign the sales contract, they are doubly-shocked: Not only does she look exactly like their other sister who had committed suicide 30 years earlier, but both were born on the same date, and went by the same nickname. 

From here on, every turn in the story makes it stranger, and eventually, even if true, it gets fantastically implausible. The fact that the director keeps filming these three old women, not in reenactment scenes, but in real time, as they uncover uncomfortable secrets from the past, was disorienting in itself. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=G-rWcxgF-E4) *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

Last year, I saw a bunch of films by wild partners Nicholas Gurewitch and Albert [Reed's son] Birney. **STRAWBERRY MANSION** (2021) is another quirky fantasy by Birney, combining elements from René Magritte, Hal Ashby's 'Being there', Michel Gondry and Daniel Clowes. It stars an Agent Cooper lookalike, and is leaning hard into the heart of weird-core. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dpPsvOSc1Gk)

*"In a future where the government records dreams and taxes them, a dream auditor gets caught up in the dreams of an aging eccentric."*

4/10 for originality, uniqueness, surrealism and not being shy of trying something completely different.

Extra: In **IT FEELS LIKE FOREVER** (2018), a lonely, long-haired dude takes you on a tour of the house he lives in. It's weird. F.ex, at one point, he's listening to a record of a dolphin who's playing the piano. The whole thing is like that. 

🍿 

**BENILDE OR THE VIRGIN MOTHER** (1975), my 3rd theatrical piece by Portuguese Manoel de Oliveira, is considered his first "real" masterpiece in the longest career of any filmmaker (He shot his first in 1927 and his last in 2015!). It's a stilted, Buñuel-lite chamber drama about an innocent young woman who was raised in seclusion, and who believes that it was God who impregnated her. This disclosure is explored in a staged, haunted style, and the truth of what really happened eludes everybody around her; the doctor, priest, the maid, her aunt, her fiance and her father. It's an acquired artificiality of a claustrophobic ambiguity. And all the while, the wind is howling outside, nearly drowning the conflicts and the agonies. 

*(More - below)*.
  - *(Continued)*

**4 MORE PALESTINIAN FILMS (FIRST 3 ARE BY ANNEMARIE JACIR):**

🍿 **WAJIB** (2017), a moving drama by Annemarie Jacir, with 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. After years of living in Italy, Saleh Bakri returns to his native Nazareth. Begrudgingly he honors his “Wajib” (or duty) to hand out invitations for his sister’s wedding, together with his estranged father (Played by real-life Mohammad Bakri). It's a small, well-crafted and nuanced relationship drama, carried on via conversations and arguments inside an old Volvo and the living rooms of the invitees. Highly recommended. **At least 8/10.** 

🍿 **WHEN I SAW YOU** (2012) is a political coming-of-age story that is seldom told sympathetically; The fate of the refugees who escaped their villages during the 1967 war and ended up in Jordanian tent camps. It is told from a defiant 11-yo child eye's view, a boy who misses his father, and runs away back home, to search for him. In the process he meets a group of Fedayeen fighters who allow him to join their ranks. The performance by the child actor was genuine and compelling. Among the 7 of Jacir's films that I saw, this is one of my favorites. 

🍿 **A FEW CRUMBS FOR THE BIRDS**, co-directed by Annemarie Jacir in 2005, is a hopeless documentary look at a forlorn tiny village in Jordan, the last stop before the Iraqi border. There's nothing there, but a few vendors selling petrol from plastic jerry cans to the lorries passing on the highway, and a few refugee girls dancing at the bar at night. The filmmakers made a stop to film it and stayed - until the Jordanian secret police put a halt to it... *[Female Director.]*

🍿 The Oscar-nominated **AVE MARIA** (2015), my first film by Basil Khalil. The silent routine of 5 Nuns living in the West Bank wilderness is disturbed when an Israeli settler family breaks down right outside the convent just as the Sabbath comes into effect.
[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8GhxEsSiums) It's not subtle at all: The three Israelis are stereo-typically terrible, entitled people. But their portrayal is also very accurate. **9/10.** 

🍿 

**COSTA-GAVRAS X 3:** 

🍿 *"Here everyone confesses. Believe me."*

**THE CONFESSION** (1970), made during his first creative peak, together with his usual cadre of friends, writer Jorge Semprún, lead actor Yves Montand and cinematographer Raoul Coutard. 

Like the Ophüls doc above, it litigates another fraught chapter in European history, the Communist show trials in Russia and the conflicting loyalties of communist sympathizers throughout the 20th century. Yves Montand is a straight-arrow vice-minister in the Czech government, who's arrested on trumped-up charges of treason together with a bunch of other loyalists. The bulk of the brutal movie is spend during his long torture period, where he is made to confess to the fabricated crimes. It's very much '1984' from the Smith arrest until the 'Room 101' scene (he is badly abused, but for some reason never beaten). This warning about authoritarianism takes place in Czechoslovakia, but it's obvious that it happens everywhere. The restored copy is superb. 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes.

🍿 *"Luxury is a right..."*

The first hour of his hi-finance corporate thriller **CAPITAL** (2012) was very promising. A newly-appointed CEO of a major French bank develops a ruthless style of amassing power and control over the board and shareholders. Very much like a second-tier, anti-capitalist version of ‘Succession’. It was also my first drama with Gad Elmaleh, the superstar comedian, as the leading character.

Unfortunately, in the middle of the suspense, the copy that I watched got corrupted, and wouldn’t play the rest. ⬇️ Could Not Finish ⬇️. *If anybody has a link to a pirated version I could borrow, please email me.*

🍿 Also, **PARTHÉNON** (2003), a CGI-short about the history of the Athenian building. It is displayed at the British Museum.

🍿 

**THE SECRET LIFE OF WALTER MITTY** (2013) is a feel-good, mainstream fantasy about a mild-mannered, ordinary office-drone who daydreams heroic escapades, while ineffectually wooing Kristen Wiig. Some of his misadventures are 'inspirational' (especially the 'Ground control to Major Tom' scene), but most were Hallmark-Mid. 

🍿 

In **1000 WOMEN IN HORROR** (2025) a group of female filmmakers discuss the unique perspective women bring to the horror genre, all the way from 1895 until 2018. It's a feminist documentary, told from a revisionist, critical gender theory, so it's politically correct and somehow insightful. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p2zLCzV9CAA)

Having absolutely zero interest in horror, I only watched it because it has 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes, but it didn't convince me one bit to give the genre another spin. The only exceptions that I found interesting were Mervin LeRoy's 'The bad seed', Kaneto Shindō' Kuruneko', and a re-watch of 'Rosemary's Baby'. 2/10. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 Georges Méliès's **A TERRIBLE NIGHT** (1896. 2 min) is a Kafkaesque nightmare about a giant bad bug. Found on the [Cinémathèque française online website, which offers 247 other rare films for free viewing.](https://www.cinematheque.fr/henri/english/) 

🍿 **WHALE VALLEY** (2013. 15 min.) takes place in a remote Icelandic farm. A young boy walks in on his older brother as he's wrapping a rope around his neck in their barn. Very few words are said, but the simple story is told perfectly. Re-watch ♻️. [**Best film of the week!**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rvPiCpyW17M)

🍿 **SONG OF AVIGNON** (1998. 9 min.), my first monologue poem by Lithuanian-American avant-garde artist Jonas Mekas, a diary film about depression very much in the style of Allen Ginsberg.

🍿 Stanley Kubrick's first color film was not 'Spartacus', but **THE SEAFARERS** (1953. 29 min.), an industrial film that was commissioned by the "Seafarers International Union", extolling the super-strong Workers Union. Not "Kubrickesque" at all, but cool. 

🍿 Taylor Ramos and Tony Zhou, of 'Every Frame a Picture', return with two new essays, done in collaboration with The Criterion Channel: [**THE BLADE : THE EDGES OF WUXIA**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ttehPZsNcxI) and **NIGHT OF THE LIVING DEAD: LIMITATIONS INTO VIRTUE**. For kicks, here's also some old ones from 2016, [**IN PRAISE OF CHAIRS**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FfGKNJ4mldE) and **SHOT / REVERSE SHOT**. Always insightful. 

🍿 

[**(ALL MY FILM REVIEWS - HERE)**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)
2. **Twilight (2008, Catherine Hardwicke) — re-watch** — Now that I’m done with the *Transformers* movies, this seemed like the most natural continuation of the “can a franchise *this* successful really be *that* bad?” theme. I actually had seen the first *Twilight* (and only the first one) before, but being as I was around 14 or 15 at the time, I figured that it might be worth revisiting it to see whether my judgement as a teenager might not have been entirely trustworthy. And, now that I’ve rewatched it, I must admit that it probably isn’t *quite* the atrocity that I insisted it was as a teenage boy. To be clear, it’s still terrible, but, having managed to endure through the first two of the books since then — I still haven’t read the latter two — I’m kind of impressed that the filmmakers managed to take such indescribably revolting source material, and turn it into something so inoffensively and unremarkably boring.

The biggest reason why the movie version of *Twilight* is so much better, or less bad, than the book is that the main characters are so much less awful. Not less awful as characters, mind — there’s still nothing remotely interesting about any of them — but, as *people*, they don’t seem to be nearly as loathsome. Bella’s internal monologue is now just pretentious pseudo-poetic nonsense, rather than being an endless list of petty grievances against anyone and anything that crosses her path, and Edward comes off as if he actually likes being around her, rather than solely being obsessed with controlling her. Even movie!Edward’s most infamously creepy moment, where he reveals that he regularly breaks into Bella’s house at night to watch her sleep, makes him come off more as a hormonal adolescent with too much power than the psychotic abuser that he came off as in the novels.

Now, I could finish by talking about all the ways that the movie version of *Twilight* is still awful.  I could, for instance, talk about the dialogue. I could talk about how the script is such a lazy hack job that they actually wrote a scene of Edward explicitly telling Bella that he is a vampire, and then put the narration explaining that Bella knows Edward is a vampire in the *following* scene, as if Melissa Rosenberg was worried the audience might have gotten amnesia during the interval. Or I could talk about the acting, and note that I think I can sort-of see what Robert Pattinson is trying to do here, but he ruins it by overplaying his awkwardness to the point that Bella falling for him feels more like an act of pity than being overwhelmed by his beauty. I could talk about what a weird mess the plot is, and how jarring it is when, after an hour and a half of almost nothing of consequence happening, the movie randomly tries to segue into a breakneck action movie in the last half-hour (though, to be fair, this too was even worse in the book). I could talk about the tacky-looking effects, and how I’m not sure anything in a visual medium could have lived up to the book’s description of the incredible beauty of vampire sparkling, but the ones here feel like they’re not even trying. But, really, what’s the point? It’s the movie version of *Twilight*. Expecting it to be actually good would be your own fault. So I will close off by acknowledging that I do actually kind of like the soundtrack, and then give it a rating of **3/10**.

**Crumb (1994, Terry Zwigoff)** — This is the first movie in a while that I seriously considered giving up on about halfway through. I ultimately decided against it, but I can’t say the experience got any better. I feel like this movie is supposed to make me think of Robert Crumb as a bold and interesting provocateur, and I don’t know whether that’s actually true or not — I have no prior knowledge of or exposure to his work outside of what’s shown in the movie — but the documentary certainly doesn’t convey it. Mostly, it seems like Zwigoff’s whole approach is to just show random grotesque images from Crumb’s work, and then let the talking heads assert that there’s some greater purpose to the images besides shock value, even though we don’t see any evidence of it. I’m not going to give this a 1/10 because it is at least not incompetently made, but I really don’t get what the appeal of this is supposed to be. **2/10**

**Close Encounters of the Third Kind (1977, Steven Spielberg) — re-watch** — Watched the director’s cut this time. Still incredible. What really fascinated me about *Close Encounters* on this viewing was how, despite its science-fiction trappings, it commits so hard and so boldly to viewing things through the lens of quasi-mystical faith. Up until the end, there really is not much hard evidence presented that the aliens are real, and, objectively, one could easily argue that Neary really is just as crazy as his wife and kids seem to be. But unlike in, say, *Rosemary’s Baby*, the movie never really feels like it’s allowing the situation to be ambiguous. The audience is expected to simply understand that Roy has had an experience, however brief, of a kind that he must not doubt, and, perhaps, *cannot* doubt. For an artist working in the modern era of science and rationalism, that Spielberg could so effectively get an audience on board with such a concept is a remarkable testament to his talents. One of his many basically-perfect works. **10/10**

**Hamlet (1996, Kenneth Branagh)** — I really liked this one. I haven’t seen very many of the numerous movie adaptations of *Hamlet* — only this, the Olivier one, and, if you count it, *The Lion King* — so I can’t say very well how this compares to others. But watching it, I find that Branagh does a good job getting at something that I’ve long seen as the most interesting part of the play, but that I have rarely seen acknowledged elsewhere: the sense that Hamlet — the character, that is, not the story — does not fit, and is not meant to fit, in the world of tragedy that he inhabits. In contrast to Olivier’s version, which explicitly commits from the beginning to the interpretation of Hamlet as “a man who couldn’t make up his mind”, Branagh’s Hamlet feels like a more protean figure, whose identity is still too unformed and unsettled for the viewer to identify any single flaw that defines him. I find this approach to be both more interesting, and also to be a much more faithful fit to the actual story of Hamlet. A must-watch. **9/10**

**Movie of the week:** *Close Encounters of the Third Kind*
3. **Tropic Thunder (2008, Ben Stiller)**

Such a ridiculous movie. Wanted something funny and stupid and this fit the bill. Jack Black, Ben Stiller, Robert Downey Jr play a group of actors forced to become their roles (soldiers in Vietnam). Funny cameo from Tom Cruise. **6/10**

**True Romance (1993, Tony Scott)**

I enjoyed this one! Star cast, I had no idea it was Patricia Arquette as Alabama until I looked up the IMDb! Couple on the run with a ton of cocaine, everything goes wrong. **7/10**

**Sixth Sense (1999, M Night Shyamalan)**

Hadn’t seen this one since it first came out and it held up pretty well. Really liked Bruce Willis and his bond with Cole, played by Haley Joel Osment. **6.5/10**
4. Michael Clayton, written and directed by Tony Gilroy and starring George Clooney. I put this movie on every few years and I still get goosebumps with the final scene. Clooney did a great job, but every actor in this movie does something special with their character, and both Tom Wilkinson and Tilda Swinton basically steal the show.
